bibliographic_data.title: Can we say a cat is a cat? Understanding the challenges in annotating physiological signal-based emotion data
bibliographic_data.abstract: Artificial Intelligence (AI) algorithms, trained on emotion data extracted from physiological signals, provide a promising approach to monitoring emotions, affect, and mental well-being. However, the field encounters challenges because there is a lack of effective methods for collecting high-quality data in everyday settings that genuinely reflect changes in emotion or affect. This paper presents a position discussion on the current technique of annotating physiological signal-based emotion data. Our discourse underscores the importance of adopting a nuanced understanding of annotation processes, paving the way for a more insightful exploration of the intricate relationship between physiological signals and human emotions.

core_content.novelty_claim: The paper discusses the challenges in annotating physiological signal-based emotion data, emphasizing the need for a nuanced understanding of annotation processes to improve AI algorithms' performance.
core_content.key_methodology: The authors present a position discussion on current techniques and challenges related to annotating physiological signal-based emotion data.
outcomes: Identified limitations in current annotation methods
Emphasized the importance of adopting a nuanced approach for annotations
core_content.full_text_markdown:
Artificial Intelligence (AI) algorithms, trained on emotion data extracted from physiological signals, provide a promising approach to monitoring emotions, affect, and mental well-being. However, the field encounters challenges because there is a lack of effective methods for collecting high-quality data in everyday settings that genuinely reflect changes in emotion or affect. This paper presents a position discussion on the current technique of annotating physiological signal-based emotion data. Our discourse underscores the importance of adopting a nuanced understanding of annotation processes, paving the way for a more insightful exploration of the intricate relationship between physiological signals and human emotions.

The paper highlights several challenges in collecting and utilizing annotations with respect to physiological signals for training emotion-affect-mental well-being recognition algorithms. These include the complexity and bias in the annotation procedure due to psychological models and emotion theories, as well as the limitations of wearable devices that do not provide raw data. The authors also discuss various methods used for annotating physiological data, such as self-assessment, expert annotations, and physiological correlation, and their respective limitations.
